stem: Buyer preview: SaaS · 22 FTE · €18K/mo spend, heavy on AWS · just closed Series-B €15M. Which archetype?
feedback_correct: Series-B + AWS-heavy + 22 FTE + founder-CEO is the Tom signature — quant-anchored opener.
feedback_incorrect: Recent funding round + engineering-led card spend + small FTE + SaaS industry is the Tom (rocket) archetype. Maria/Emma/Lukas don't carry a fresh-funding signal.

stem: You're calling Lukas — DE restaurant group, 4 locations, top objection 'I don't trust new fintechs.' Best-fit prop to lead with?

options.text: Prop 1 — Real-time spend control + per-card limits

feedback_correct: Lukas's pain is expense fraud + spend visibility across 4 sites. Real-time spend control (Prop 1) plus per-card limits (Prop 2 supports) directly answers it. Trust is built by demonstrating visibility, not by pitching FX.
feedback_incorrect: Lukas's lead pain is fraud + visibility — Prop 1 (real-time spend control) is the lead. Prop 3 (FX) only enters as a secondary frame on the Italian-supplier line.

stem: You ran M1+M2+M3 cleanly against Maria (retail UK) — same shape against Lukas (DE restaurant) but the call ended cold. Most likely cause?

options.text: Lukas needs a quant-anchored signal-statement, not a behavioural one — you didn't switch the opener style.
You should have pitched Prop 4 instead of Prop 1.
M3 calendar close doesn't work in German.

feedback_correct: UK buyers respond to behavioural openers ('who approves what'); DE buyers respond to quant-anchored signals (FX volume, headcount, monthly spend) — per L.D. §10.2. The moves are the same; the framing is the buyer's.
feedback_incorrect: M1/M2/M3 are stable across the archetypes; what flexes is the diagnostic framing (UK behavioural vs DE quant per §10.2). Same move, different anchor.
